{"Question": "How do I initialize a moving aggregation pipeline with my pandas DataFrame?", "Context": "I have a pandas DataFrame with time series data and I want to perform moving average calculations on it.", "Code_Example": "import pandas as pd
from movingaverages import MovingAggrPipe

# Load your data
time_series_data = pd.read_csv('stock_data.csv')

# Initialize the moving aggregation pipeline with your data
moving_pipeline = MovingAggrPipe.from_dataframe(time_series_data)

# Now the pipeline is ready for moving average calculations
print("Pipeline initialized with data shape:", moving_pipeline.data.shape)", "Usage": "MovingAggrPipe.from_dataframe(df)"}

metadata.description: Initialize a moving aggregation pipeline with a DataFrame

page_content:
{"Question": "How do I calculate simple moving averages for my time series data?", "Context": "I have stock price data and want to calculate a 20-day simple moving average to identify trends.", "Code_Example": "from movingaverages import MovingAggrPipe, moving_average

# Create pipeline with your data
pipeline = MovingAggrPipe.from_dataframe(stock_df)

# Calculate simple moving average
sma_results = pipeline | moving_average(
    columns='close_price',
    window=20,
    method='simple'
)

# Check the results
print(f"Simple moving average calculated for {sma_results.data['close_price_ma'].notna().sum()} data points")
print(f"Latest SMA: {sma_results.data['close_price_ma'].iloc[-1]:.2f}")

# Compare with original price
latest_price = sma_results.data['close_price'].iloc[-1]
latest_sma = sma_results.data['close_price_ma'].iloc[-1]
print(f"Current price: {latest_price:.2f}, 20-day SMA: {latest_sma:.2f}")", "Usage": "moving_average(columns='close_price', window=20, method='simple')"}

{"Question": "How can I calculate exponential and weighted moving averages for more responsive signals?", "Context": "I want to compare different moving average types - simple, exponential, and weighted - to see which responds faster to price changes.", "Code_Example": "from movingaverages import MovingAggrPipe, moving_average

# Calculate different types of moving averages
ma_comparison = pipeline | moving_average(
    columns='close_price',
    window=20,
    method='simple',
    output_suffix='_sma'
) | moving_average(
    columns='close_price',
    window=20,
    method='exponential',
    output_suffix='_ema'
) | moving_average(
    columns='close_price',
    window=20,
    method='weighted',
    output_suffix='_wma'
)

# Compare responsiveness
print("Moving Average Comparison:")
print(f"Simple MA: {ma_comparison.data['close_price_sma'].iloc[-1]:.2f}")
print(f"Exponential MA: {ma_comparison.data['close_price_ema'].iloc[-1]:.2f}")
print(f"Weighted MA: {ma_comparison.data['close_price_wma'].iloc[-1]:.2f}")

# Calculate responsiveness (how much each MA changed in last 5 periods)
for ma_type in ['sma', 'ema', 'wma']:
    col = f"close_price_{ma_type}"
    change = ma_comparison.data[col].iloc[-1] - ma_comparison.data[col].iloc[-6]
    print(f"{ma_type.upper()} 5-period change: {change:.2f}")", "Usage": "moving_average(columns='close_price', window=20, method='exponential', output_suffix='_ema')"}

{"Question": "How do I calculate moving variance and standard deviation for volatility analysis?", "Context": "I want to analyze the volatility of my time series data using rolling variance and standard deviation.", "Code_Example": "from movingaverages import MovingAggrPipe, moving_variance

# Calculate moving variance and standard deviation
volatility_analysis = pipeline | moving_variance(
    columns=['close_price', 'volume'],
    window=30,
    var_suffix='_var',
    std_suffix='_std'
)

# Check volatility metrics
print("Volatility Analysis:")
print(f"Price variance: {volatility_analysis.data['close_price_var'].iloc[-1]:.2f}")
print(f"Price std dev: {volatility_analysis.data['close_price_std'].iloc[-1]:.2f}")
print(f"Volume variance: {volatility_analysis.data['volume_var'].iloc[-1]:.2f}")

# Identify high volatility periods
high_volatility = volatility_analysis.data[volatility_analysis.data['close_price_std'] > volatility_analysis.data['close_price_std'].quantile(0.9)]
print(f"High volatility periods: {len(high_volatility)} data points")", "Usage": "moving_variance(columns=['close_price', 'volume'], window=30, var_suffix='_var', std_suffix='_std')"}

metadata.description: Calculate moving variance and standard deviation

page_content:
{"Question": "How can I calculate moving quantiles to understand data distribution over time?", "Context": "I want to track the 25th, 50th, and 75th percentiles of my data over time to understand how the distribution changes.", "Code_Example": "from movingaverages import MovingAggrPipe, moving_quantile

# Calculate moving quantiles
quantile_analysis = pipeline | moving_quantile(
    columns='close_price',
    quantile=[0.25, 0.5, 0.75],
    window=60
)

# Check the distribution metrics
print("Moving Quantiles (60-day window):")
print(f"25th percentile: {quantile_analysis.data['close_price_q0.25'].iloc[-1]:.2f}")
print(f"50th percentile (median): {quantile_analysis.data['close_price_q0.5'].iloc[-1]:.2f}")
print(f"75th percentile: {quantile_analysis.data['close_price_q0.75'].iloc[-1]:.2f}")

# Calculate interquartile range
iqr = quantile_analysis.data['close_price_q0.75'].iloc[-1] - quantile_analysis.data['close_price_q0.25'].iloc[-1]
print(f"Interquartile Range: {iqr:.2f}")

# Identify outliers using IQR method
current_price = quantile_analysis.data['close_price'].iloc[-1]
if current_price > quantile_analysis.data['close_price_q0.75'].iloc[-1] + 1.5 * iqr:
    print("Current price is above upper outlier threshold")", "Usage": "moving_quantile(columns='close_price', quantile=[0.25, 0.5, 0.75], window=60)"}

metadata.description: Calculate moving quantiles for distribution analysis

page_content:
{"Question": "How do I calculate moving correlations between different time series?", "Context": "I want to track how the correlation between stock price and volume changes over time.", "Code_Example": "from movingaverages import MovingAggrPipe, moving_correlation

# Calculate moving correlation between price and volume
correlation_analysis = pipeline | moving_correlation(
    column_pairs=[('close_price', 'volume'), ('close_price', 'high_price')],
    window=30
)

# Check correlation results
print("Moving Correlations (30-day window):")
print(f"Price-Volume correlation: {correlation_analysis.data['close_price_volume_corr'].iloc[-1]:.3f}")
print(f"Price-High correlation: {correlation_analysis.data['close_price_high_price_corr'].iloc[-1]:.3f}")

# Identify periods of strong correlation
strong_corr = correlation_analysis.data[abs(correlation_analysis.data['close_price_volume_corr']) > 0.7]
print(f"Periods with strong price-volume correlation: {len(strong_corr)} data points")

# Check correlation stability
corr_std = correlation_analysis.data['close_price_volume_corr'].std()
print(f"Correlation stability (std dev): {corr_std:.3f}")", "Usage": "moving_correlation(column_pairs=[('close_price', 'volume'), ('close_price', 'high_price')], window=30)"}

metadata.description: Calculate moving correlations between time series

page_content:
{"Question": "How can I calculate moving z-scores to identify unusual data points?", "Context": "I want to identify when my data points are unusually high or low relative to recent history using z-scores.", "Code_Example": "from movingaverages import MovingAggrPipe, moving_zscore

# Calculate moving z-scores
zscore_analysis = pipeline | moving_zscore(
    columns=['close_price', 'volume'],
    window=30
)

# Check current z-scores
print("Current Z-Scores (30-day window):")
print(f"Price z-score: {zscore_analysis.data['close_price_zscore'].iloc[-1]:.2f}")
print(f"Volume z-score: {zscore_analysis.data['volume_zscore'].iloc[-1]:.2f}")

# Identify extreme values
extreme_price = abs(zscore_analysis.data['close_price_zscore'].iloc[-1]) > 2
extreme_volume = abs(zscore_analysis.data['volume_zscore'].iloc[-1]) > 2

if extreme_price:
    print("Price is unusually high/low (>2 std devs from mean)")
if extreme_volume:
    print("Volume is unusually high/low (>2 std devs from mean)")

# Count extreme events in last 100 periods
recent_extremes = zscore_analysis.data.tail(100)
price_extremes = (abs(recent_extremes['close_price_zscore']) > 2).sum()
print(f"Price extreme events in last 100 periods: {price_extremes}")", "Usage": "moving_zscore(columns=['close_price', 'volume'], window=30)"}

metadata.description: Calculate moving z-scores for outlier detection

page_content:
{"Question": "How do I detect turning points (peaks and troughs) in my time series data?", "Context": "I want to identify local peaks and troughs in my data to find potential reversal points.", "Code_Example": "from movingaverages import MovingAggrPipe, detect_turning_points

# Detect turning points
turning_points = pipeline | detect_turning_points(
    columns='close_price',
    window=15,
    threshold=2.0  # Minimum 2% change to consider a turning point
)

# Check turning point results
peaks = turning_points.data[turning_points.data['close_price_peak']]
troughs = turning_points.data[turning_points.data['close_price_trough']]

print(f"Peaks detected: {len(peaks)}")
print(f"Troughs detected: {len(troughs)}")

# Show recent turning points
recent_peaks = peaks.tail(5)[['timestamp', 'close_price']]
recent_troughs = troughs.tail(5)[['timestamp', 'close_price']]

print("Recent peaks:")
print(recent_peaks)
print("Recent troughs:")
print(recent_troughs)

# Calculate average time between turning points
if len(peaks) > 1:
    peak_intervals = peaks['timestamp'].diff().dt.days.mean()
    print(f"Average days between peaks: {peak_intervals:.1f}")", "Usage": "detect_turning_points(columns='close_price', window=15, threshold=2.0)"}

{"Question": "How can I calculate moving ratios between different metrics?", "Context": "I want to track the ratio of high price to low price over time to measure price volatility.", "Code_Example": "from movingaverages import MovingAggrPipe, moving_ratio

# Calculate moving ratio of high to low price
ratio_analysis = pipeline | moving_ratio(
    numerator_column='high_price',
    denominator_column='low_price',
    window=20,
    output_column='high_low_ratio'
)

# Check the ratio results
print("High-Low Price Ratio Analysis:")
print(f"Current ratio: {ratio_analysis.data['high_low_ratio'].iloc[-1]:.3f}")
print(f"Average ratio: {ratio_analysis.data['high_low_ratio'].mean():.3f}")

# Identify periods of high volatility (high ratio)
high_volatility = ratio_analysis.data[ratio_analysis.data['high_low_ratio'] > 1.05]
print(f"High volatility periods: {len(high_volatility)} data points")

# Calculate price efficiency ratio (close to high ratio)
efficiency_analysis = pipeline | moving_ratio(
    numerator_column='close_price',
    denominator_column='high_price',
    window=20,
    output_column='close_high_ratio'
)

print(f"Current close/high ratio: {efficiency_analysis.data['close_high_ratio'].iloc[-1]:.3f}")", "Usage": "moving_ratio(numerator_column='high_price', denominator_column='low_price', window=20, output_column='high_low_ratio')"}

{"Question": "How do I calculate time-weighted averages for more recent data emphasis?", "Context": "I want to calculate moving averages that give more weight to recent observations using exponential decay.", "Code_Example": "from movingaverages import MovingAggrPipe, time_weighted_average

# Calculate time-weighted average with exponential decay
twa_analysis = pipeline | time_weighted_average(
    columns='close_price',
    window=30,
    decay_factor=0.9  # Higher values give more weight to recent data
)

# Compare with simple moving average
comparison = twa_analysis | moving_average(
    columns='close_price',
    window=30,
    method='simple',
    output_suffix='_sma'
)

print("Time-Weighted vs Simple Moving Average:")
print(f"Time-weighted average: {comparison.data['close_price_twa'].iloc[-1]:.2f}")
print(f"Simple moving average: {comparison.data['close_price_sma'].iloc[-1]:.2f}")

# Check responsiveness to recent changes
recent_change = comparison.data['close_price'].iloc[-1] - comparison.data['close_price'].iloc[-5]
twa_change = comparison.data['close_price_twa'].iloc[-1] - comparison.data['close_price_twa'].iloc[-5]
sma_change = comparison.data['close_price_sma'].iloc[-1] - comparison.data['close_price_sma'].iloc[-5]

print(f"Recent price change: {recent_change:.2f}")
print(f"TWA response: {twa_change:.2f}")
print(f"SMA response: {sma_change:.2f}")", "Usage": "time_weighted_average(columns='close_price', window=30, decay_factor=0.9)"}

metadata.description: Calculate time-weighted averages with exponential decay
Apply custom aggregation functions to moving windows

page_content:
{"Question": "How do I chain multiple moving average analyses together?", "Context": "I want to apply multiple moving average calculations in sequence to create a comprehensive technical analysis.", "Code_Example": "from movingaverages import (
    MovingAggrPipe, 
    moving_average,
    moving_variance,
    moving_zscore,
    detect_turning_points
)

# Chain multiple analyses together
comprehensive_analysis = (
    MovingAggrPipe.from_dataframe(stock_data)
    | moving_average(
        columns='close_price',
        window=20,
        method='simple',
        output_suffix='_sma20'
    )
    | moving_average(
        columns='close_price',
        window=50,
        method='simple',
        output_suffix='_sma50'
    )
    | moving_variance(
        columns='close_price',
        window=30,
        std_suffix='_std30'
    )
    | moving_zscore(
        columns='close_price',
        window=30
    )
    | detect_turning_points(
        columns='close_price',
        window=15,
        threshold=1.5
    )
)

# Check all calculated metrics
print("Comprehensive Moving Average Analysis:")
print(f"20-day SMA: {comprehensive_analysis.data['close_price_sma20'].iloc[-1]:.2f}")
print(f"50-day SMA: {comprehensive_analysis.data['close_price_sma50'].iloc[-1]:.2f}")
print(f"30-day Std Dev: {comprehensive_analysis.data['close_price_std30'].iloc[-1]:.2f}")
print(f"Z-score: {comprehensive_analysis.data['close_price_zscore'].iloc[-1]:.2f}")

# Check for golden cross (20-day SMA crosses above 50-day SMA)
current_20sma = comprehensive_analysis.data['close_price_sma20'].iloc[-1]
current_50sma = comprehensive_analysis.data['close_price_sma50'].iloc[-1]
prev_20sma = comprehensive_analysis.data['close_price_sma20'].iloc[-2]
prev_50sma = comprehensive_analysis.data['close_price_sma50'].iloc[-2]

if prev_20sma <= prev_50sma and current_20sma > current_50sma:
    print("Golden Cross detected!")", "Usage": "MovingAggrPipe.from_dataframe(data) | moving_average(...) | moving_variance(...) | moving_zscore(...) | detect_turning_points(...)"}

metadata.description: Chain multiple moving average analyses together

page_content:
{"Question": "How do I handle grouped data for moving averages across different categories?", "Context": "I have data from multiple stocks or sectors and want to calculate moving averages within each group separately.", "Code_Example": "from movingaverages import MovingAggrPipe, moving_average, moving_variance

# Calculate moving averages separately for each stock
grouped_analysis = pipeline | moving_average(
    columns='close_price',
    window=20,
    method='simple',
    group_columns=['stock_symbol', 'sector']
)

# Check results by group
print("Moving Averages by Stock and Sector:")
for group in grouped_analysis.data.groupby(['stock_symbol', 'sector']):
    stock, sector = group[0]
    group_data = group[1]
    latest_ma = group_data['close_price_ma'].iloc[-1]
    latest_price = group_data['close_price'].iloc[-1]
    print(f"{stock} ({sector}): Price={latest_price:.2f}, 20-day MA={latest_ma:.2f}")

# Calculate volatility by sector
volatility_by_sector = pipeline | moving_variance(
    columns='close_price',
    window=30,
    group_columns=['sector'],
    std_suffix='_std'
)

# Compare sector volatility
sector_volatility = volatility_by_sector.data.groupby('sector')['close_price_std'].mean()
print("\nAverage volatility by sector:")
print(sector_volatility.sort_values(ascending=False))", "Usage": "moving_average(columns='close_price', window=20, method='simple', group_columns=['stock_symbol', 'sector'])"}

metadata.description: Handle grouped data for category-specific moving averages

page_content:
{"Question": "How can I create custom visualizations from moving average results?", "Context": "I want to create charts and plots to visualize the moving averages and their relationships with the original data.", "Code_Example": "import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
from movingaverages import MovingAggrPipe, moving_average, moving_variance

# Run moving average analysis
results = (
    MovingAggrPipe.from_dataframe(stock_data)
    | moving_average(
        columns='close_price',
        window=20,
        method='simple',
        output_suffix='_sma20'
    )
    | moving_average(
        columns='close_price',
        window=50,
        method='simple',
        output_suffix='_sma50'
    )
    | moving_variance(
        columns='close_price',
        window=30,
        std_suffix='_std'
    )
)

# Create comprehensive visualization
fig, axes = plt.subplots(3, 1, figsize=(15, 12))

# Plot 1: Price with moving averages
axes[0].plot(results.data['timestamp'], results.data['close_price'], alpha=0.7, label='Close Price')
axes[0].plot(results.data['timestamp'], results.data['close_price_sma20'], label='20-day SMA', linewidth=2)
axes[0].plot(results.data['timestamp'], results.data['close_price_sma50'], label='50-day SMA', linewidth=2)
axes[0].set_title('Stock Price with Moving Averages')
axes[0].legend()
axes[0].grid(True, alpha=0.3)

# Plot 2: Volatility (standard deviation)
axes[1].plot(results.data['timestamp'], results.data['close_price_std'], color='orange')
axes[1].set_title('30-day Rolling Volatility')
axes[1].set_ylabel('Standard Deviation')
axes[1].grid(True, alpha=0.3)

# Plot 3: Price relative to moving averages
price_ratio_20 = results.data['close_price'] / results.data['close_price_sma20']
price_ratio_50 = results.data['close_price'] / results.data['close_price_sma50']
axes[2].plot(results.data['timestamp'], price_ratio_20, label='Price/20-day SMA')
axes[2].plot(results.data['timestamp'], price_ratio_50, label='Price/50-day SMA')
axes[2].axhline(y=1, color='black', linestyle='--', alpha=0.5)
axes[2].set_title('Price Relative to Moving Averages')
axes[2].set_ylabel('Ratio')
axes[2].legend()
axes[2].grid(True, alpha=0.3)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('moving_average_analysis.png', dpi=300, bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()

# Create correlation heatmap of moving averages
ma_columns = ['close_price_sma20', 'close_price_sma50', 'close_price_std']
correlation_matrix = results.data[ma_columns].corr()

plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))
sns.heatmap(correlation_matrix, annot=True, cmap='coolwarm', center=0)
plt.title('Moving Average Correlations')
plt.show()", "Usage": "plt.plot(results.data['timestamp'], results.data['close_price']); plt.plot(results.data['timestamp'], results.data['close_price_sma20'], label='20-day SMA')"}

metadata.description: Create custom visualizations for moving average results

page_content:
{"Question": "How do I get a summary of my moving average calculations?", "Context": "After running multiple moving average analyses, I want to understand the overall results and key statistics.", "Code_Example": "from movingaverages import MovingAggrPipe, moving_average, moving_variance, moving_zscore

# Run comprehensive moving average analysis
analysis = (
    MovingAggrPipe.from_dataframe(stock_data)
    | moving_average(columns='close_price', window=20, method='simple')
    | moving_variance(columns='close_price', window=30)
    | moving_zscore(columns='close_price', window=30)
)

# Get summary of moving metrics
print("Moving Average Analysis Summary:")
for metric_name, metric_info in analysis.moving_metrics.items():
    print(f"- {metric_name}: {metric_info['type']} (columns: {metric_info.get('columns', 'N/A')})")

# Get summary statistics for calculated metrics
summary_stats = analysis.data[['close_price_ma', 'close_price_var', 'close_price_std', 'close_price_zscore']].describe()
print("\nSummary Statistics:")
print(summary_stats)

# Check data quality
total_points = len(analysis.data)
valid_ma = analysis.data['close_price_ma'].notna().sum()
valid_var = analysis.data['close_price_var'].notna().sum()
valid_zscore = analysis.data['close_price_zscore'].notna().sum()

print(f"\nData Quality:")
print(f"Total data points: {total_points}")
print(f"Valid MA values: {valid_ma} ({valid_ma/total_points*100:.1f}%)")
print(f"Valid variance values: {valid_var} ({valid_var/total_points*100:.1f}%)")
print(f"Valid z-score values: {valid_zscore} ({valid_zscore/total_points*100:.1f}%)")

# Get current values
print(f"\nCurrent Values:")
print(f"Price: {analysis.data['close_price'].iloc[-1]:.2f}")
print(f"20-day MA: {analysis.data['close_price_ma'].iloc[-1]:.2f}")
print(f"30-day Std Dev: {analysis.data['close_price_std'].iloc[-1]:.2f}")
print(f"Z-score: {analysis.data['close_price_zscore'].iloc[-1]:.2f}")", "Usage": "analysis.moving_metrics for metric summary; analysis.data.describe() for statistics"}

metadata.description: Get comprehensive summary of moving average results

page_content:
{"Question": "How can I use moving averages for trading signal generation?", "Context": "I want to create trading signals based on moving average crossovers and other technical indicators.", "Code_Example": "from movingaverages import MovingAggrPipe, moving_average, moving_variance

# Create trading signal analysis
trading_signals = (
    MovingAggrPipe.from_dataframe(stock_data)
    | moving_average(
        columns='close_price',
        window=10,
        method='simple',
        output_suffix='_fast'
    )
    | moving_average(
        columns='close_price',
        window=30,
        method='simple',
        output_suffix='_slow'
    )
    | moving_variance(
        columns='close_price',
        window=20,
        std_suffix='_volatility'
    )
)

# Generate trading signals
data = trading_signals.data
data['signal'] = 0

# Golden cross (fast MA crosses above slow MA)
golden_cross = (data['close_price_fast'] > data['close_price_slow']) & 
               (data['close_price_fast'].shift(1) <= data['close_price_slow'].shift(1))
data.loc[golden_cross, 'signal'] = 1

# Death cross (fast MA crosses below slow MA)
death_cross = (data['close_price_fast'] < data['close_price_slow']) & 
              (data['close_price_fast'].shift(1) >= data['close_price_slow'].shift(1))
data.loc[death_cross, 'signal'] = -1

# Volatility-based filter
low_volatility = data['close_price_volatility'] < data['close_price_volatility'].quantile(0.3)
data.loc[low_volatility, 'signal'] = 0

# Check recent signals
recent_signals = data[data['signal'] != 0].tail(10)
print("Recent Trading Signals:")
for idx, row in recent_signals.iterrows():
    signal_type = "BUY" if row['signal'] == 1 else "SELL"
    print(f"{row['timestamp']}: {signal_type} signal at {row['close_price']:.2f}")

# Calculate signal performance
total_signals = (data['signal'] != 0).sum()
buy_signals = (data['signal'] == 1).sum()
sell_signals = (data['signal'] == -1).sum()

print(f"\nSignal Summary:")
print(f"Total signals: {total_signals}")
print(f"Buy signals: {buy_signals}")
print(f"Sell signals: {sell_signals}")", "Usage": "moving_average(columns='close_price', window=10, method='simple', output_suffix='_fast') | moving_average(columns='close_price', window=30, method='simple', output_suffix='_slow')"}

metadata.description: Use moving averages for trading signal generation
